concepts.title: Magnetic Dipole Moment of Uniform Charged Rotating Ring
concepts.conceptBody:
Calculating the Magnetic Dipole Moment of Rotating Charges 

 

In the diagram shown the ring carrys uniformly distributed charge $\text{Q}$ and it is rotating with constant angular velocity $\omega $.

Time taken by the ring to complete one revolution will be$\frac{2\pi }{\text{w}}$.

With respect to an observer standing in the state of rest, the charge $\text{Q}$ will cross him in $\frac{2\pi }{\text{w}}$.

$\text{i}=\frac{\theta }{2\pi /\text{w}}$

Magnetic moment $M=\pi {{R}^{2}}i=\pi {{R}^{2}}\frac{\theta }{2\pi /w}$

If the ring has uniformly distributed mass $m$, then the angular momentum of the ring will be $\text{M}{{\text{R}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ W}$.

$\mathrm{L}=\mathrm{Iw}$

$\mathrm{L}=\mathrm{mR}^2 \omega$

*If mass and charge are both distributed in the same manner then the value of $\frac{\mu }{\text{L}}$ is always $\frac{\theta }{2\text{M}}$.

concepts.title: Magnetic Dipole Moment of Uniform Charged Disc
concepts.conceptBody:
Magnetic Dipole Moment of Uniform Charged Disc 

Consider a disc of radius $\text{R}$ carrying uniformly distributed charge $\text{Q}$ on it. We have to find out magnetic moments.

$\begin{matrix} \frac{\text{M}}{\text{L}}=\frac{\text{Q}}{2\text{ m}} \\ \frac{\text{M}}{\frac{\text{m}{{\text{R}}^{2}}}{2}\text{w}}=\frac{\text{Q}}{2\text{ m}} \end{matrix}$

 

Example

A square current loop is placed in a uniform magnetic field as shown which makes an angle of 45 with the $\text{x}$-axis. Mass of each wire side $=\text{m}$, and length l. Find: (i) Net torque acting on the loop and find its direction. (ii) Angular displacement in the small-time t. Neglect the variation in torque during the entire time instant. 

 

Solution: 

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{B}}{\sqrt{2}}(\overline{\mathrm{i}}+\overline{\mathrm{j}}), \quad \overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}}=\mathrm{il}^2(\overline{\mathrm{k}})$

$\therefore \quad \vec{\tau}=(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{M}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{B}})$

$\left.=\frac{i \mathrm{Bl}{ }^2}{\sqrt{2}}[\overline{(\mathrm{i}}+\overline{\mathrm{j}}) \times \overline{\mathrm{k}}\right]$

$\vec{\tau }=\frac{\text{iB}{{\text{l}}^{2}}}{\sqrt{2}}\left[ \left( \overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,-\overset{}{\mathop{\text{j}}}\, \right) \right]$ 

$\therefore \left| {\vec{\tau }} \right|=\text{iB}{{\text{l}}^{2}}$

$\because$ torque is constant,

$\therefore \alpha =\left( \frac{\tau }{\text{I}} \right)=\frac{\text{iB}{{\text{l}}^{2}}}{{{\text{I}}_{\text{QS}}}}=\left( \frac{3\text{iB}}{2\text{ m}} \right)$

$\left[ {{\text{I}}_{\text{QS}}}=\frac{1}{2}\left[ \left( \frac{\text{m}{{\text{l}}^{2}}}{12} \right)+\left( \frac{\text{m}{{\text{l}}^{2}}}{4} \right)\times 4 \right]=\frac{2}{3}\text{m}{{\text{l}}^{2}} \right]$

$\therefore $ Angular displacement in the time $\text{t}$

$\theta =\frac{1}{2}\alpha \Delta{{\text{t}}^{2}}=\frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{3\text{iB}}{2\text{ m}} \right)\Delta{{\text{t}}^{2}}=\left( \frac{3\text{iB}}{4\text{ m}} \right)\Delta{{\text{t}}^{2}}$

Note: In the question if do not neglect the variation in the torque, then,

$\begin{array}{l} \tau =\left( \text{MBcos}\theta \right) \\ \text{w}\left( \frac{\text{dw}}{\text{d}\theta } \right)=\frac{\text{MBcos}\theta }{\text{I}} \\ \mathop{\int }_{0}^{\text{w}}\left( \text{wdw} \right)=\frac{\text{MB}}{\text{I}}\mathop{\int }_{0}^{\theta }\text{cos}\theta \text{d}\theta \\ \therefore \left( \frac{{{\text{w}}^{2}}}{2} \right)=\frac{\text{MB}}{\text{I}}\text{sin}\theta  \end{array}$
